thread_tweets.text:
If they keep buying more $EBAY exposure $GME shares can fall way below cash value on dilution fears.

If they are buying back shares are $10-15 then the buybacks increase the likelihood of an $EBAY purchase, not hinder it.

The convertible note hedging at $21 also matters. Below $21 market makers will be forced to sell $GME shares into the selling pressure.

They could repurchase 40% of all shares if $GME collapses to $10, their cash value per share would skyrocket, and then the $EBAY deal becomes attractive.

I built a whole thread on this two weeks ago and it’s in my drafts. This announcement just confirms the thoughts I already had. 

I’ll drop it in the next week or two

Been waiting for this from $GME.

This in my opinion confirms that GameStop Warrants will close ITM. $GME.WS

$2B repurchase program is enough to push $GME shares above $32 and exercise warrants which would raise $1.9B for GameStop.

They will likely keep degen buying $EBAY and letting shorts pile in on both $GME shares and warrants. 

If price goes to $10-15 dollars then $GME uses this $2B to buyback shares below their cash value.

They later sell those same shares they just bought back for $32 by forcing warrants to exercise. 

Cash value per share will skyrocket the $GME price floor without dilution. If played correctly then $EBAY & $GME deal will eventually look too attractive for $EBAY to pass up.

Chess not checkers. ♟️

@dankourny Check back in 6 months.

The above is the exact play by play of how this will play out.

$GME can raise hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars while reducing their share count.  Zero chance they don’t do it and this announcement confirms it.

If they keep buying more $EBAY exposure $GME shares can fall way below cash value on dilution fears.

If they are buying back shares are $10-15 then the buybacks increase the likelihood of an $EBAY purchase, not hinder it.

The convertible note hedging at $21 also matters. Below $21 market makers will be forced to sell $GME shares into the selling pressure.

They could repurchase 40% of all shares if $GME collapses to $10, their cash value per share would skyrocket, and then the $EBAY deal becomes attractive.

I built a whole thread on this two weeks ago and it’s in my drafts. This announcement just confirms the thoughts I already had. 

I’ll drop it in the next week or two

tweets.text:
The market for tokenized real-world assets just hit a real milestone, but the data shows we're earlier than the hype suggests. Quick breakdown:

• Tokenized assets (excluding stablecoins) crossed $30B last month and now sit near $34B. That's 10x growth in under two years, up from less than $3B in mid-2024.

• The jump wasn't random. The GENIUS Act gave US stablecoins clearer rules, onchain infrastructure matured, and big financial institutions moved from testing to live systems all at once.

• Most of that growth came from one place: US Treasurys. Investors get a familiar yield-bearing asset in a faster, digital form, and idle stablecoins suddenly have somewhere to earn.

• Today, tokenized Treasurys and gold make up roughly two-thirds of the entire market. Gold alone is basically the whole commodity category, $5B out of $5.1B.

• It's spreading across chains, not consolidating. Ethereum still leads with half the market ($15.7B), followed by BNB Chain ($4B), Solana ($2.2B), Stellar ($1.7B), and Liquid.

• Here's the catch nobody talks about: most of this isn't actually being used. Only ~5% of the $15.2B in tokenized bonds touches DeFi. The rest just sits onchain as digital receipts, not composable financial tools.

• So while forecasts all point way up (McKinsey says $2-4T, Standard Chartered $30T+ by 2034), today's market is still ~0.01% of global bonds. The easy part (moving assets onchain) is done. The hard part (making them actually work together) is just starting.
tweets.full_text:
The market for tokenized real-world assets just hit a real milestone, but the data shows we're earlier than the hype suggests. Quick breakdown:

• Tokenized assets (excluding stablecoins) crossed $30B last month and now sit near $34B. That's 10x growth in under two years, up from less than $3B in mid-2024.

• The jump wasn't random. The GENIUS Act gave US stablecoins clearer rules, onchain infrastructure matured, and big financial institutions moved from testing to live systems all at once.

• Most of that growth came from one place: US Treasurys. Investors get a familiar yield-bearing asset in a faster, digital form, and idle stablecoins suddenly have somewhere to earn.

• Today, tokenized Treasurys and gold make up roughly two-thirds of the entire market. Gold alone is basically the whole commodity category, $5B out of $5.1B.

• It's spreading across chains, not consolidating. Ethereum still leads with half the market ($15.7B), followed by BNB Chain ($4B), Solana ($2.2B), Stellar ($1.7B), and Liquid.

• Here's the catch nobody talks about: most of this isn't actually being used. Only ~5% of the $15.2B in tokenized bonds touches DeFi. The rest just sits onchain as digital receipts, not composable financial tools.

• So while forecasts all point way up (McKinsey says $2-4T, Standard Chartered $30T+ by 2034), today's market is still ~0.01% of global bonds. The easy part (moving assets onchain) is done. The hard part (making them actually work together) is just starting.

tweets.text:
been wondering for a long time why Treasury had exempt Nobitex. it was a known fact that Iran had active crypto exchanges. 

I suspect but don't know for sure that Treasury was trying to balance allowing individual Iranians to get out of the Rial (hence depreciating the currency and hurting the regime) with the possibility of the IRGC using stablecoins to fund themselves.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Today, Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control designated Nobitex, Iran’s largest digital asset exchange, along with three other Iranian digital asset exchanges, as part of Economic Fury and the Trump Administration’s efforts to eliminate the threat posed by the Iranian
tweets.full_text:
been wondering for a long time why Treasury had exempt Nobitex. it was a known fact that Iran had active crypto exchanges. 

I suspect but don't know for sure that Treasury was trying to balance allowing individual Iranians to get out of the Rial (hence depreciating the currency and hurting the regime) with the possibility of the IRGC using stablecoins to fund themselves.

tweets.text:
I could not disagree more here. 

First of all, moral certainty and being assured of your own moral superiority (as EAs and consequentialists often are) tends to result in great harms done. All the great crimes of history have been justified by the purported moral superiority of those committing it. You can object to this, but in my view most EAs are committed consequentialists, which generally involves overlooking some near term harms in service of long term utility or goodness. It is a philosophy that by its very nature justifies harms that would otherwise be abhorrent to us. 

Second, I am much more cautious of someone who has a high degree of moral certainty than I would be of someone who is a skeptic and defaults to their own intuitions. The former is much more likely to justify atrocities. As CS Lewis said: "Of all tyrannies, a tyranny sincerely exercised for the good of its victims may be the most oppressive." 

Third, the outward appearance of virtue is often a tactic used by an individual aiming to conceal a much greater vice. We have an existing high profile example of this in the EA world: SBF's campaign to present himself as a highly moral consequentialist vegan boy genius. Or see predators like Jimmy Savvile being very active philanthropists. 

You could say I am smuggling in the assumption that EAs and the Anthropic crowd specifically have higher moral certainty, as opposed to just "a tendency to think more about morality". I stand by this. I have seen them in action. I read all their literature. They seem committed to the notion that a single firm should be in charge of the  model that the whole world consults, and that firm should be them, and that they should be solely tasked with "aligning" the model (as in: defining the moral framework that the model uses), and the personally principally tasked with that alignment should be an Anthropic employee, namely Amanda Askell. 

Anthropic already has imbued their models with latent racial preference [1] a general liberal bias [2] and a progressivism that exceeds that of any single country [3]. it stands to reason that the "philanthropic" activity by these people will follow these same value patterns. If I had to guess, the philanthropy these people engage in will follow the pattern of Mackenzie Bezos - done sloppily, at massive scale, with little oversight, and funding questionable ultra-progressive organizations. 

The control that AI firms and their executives wield over society is an existential question and it will not be settled in a manner that the EAs long for. It is politically intolerable for these companies to grow into valuations in the tens of trillions while most workers are laid off. Datacenters will burn and their equity will be expropriated long before this happens. 

[1] https://t.co/rAJX7wQJWh
[2] https://t.co/zgyhrQWiAi
[3] https://t.co/gwAilisnTX

thread_tweets.text:
seems like there are a few out there, but i would wager it's an extremely small % of people that are true sealed collectors and just want to hold the sealed product vs those who want to buy sealed in order to flip/sell

and it's a timeframe thing too, if you buy sealed to sell the next week you're a "scalper", if you buy sealed to sell in a decade you're an "investor", if you buy sealed to "collect" but "might one day sell in the future" then I guess you're a collector?

tweets.text:
It’s kind of insane that some coins are actually up over the last few days, in the face of an enormous BTC dump and mass fear/panic over saylor and quantum

Bullish coins decorrelating from Bitcoin, bullish coins holding up despite an overall crash

Heck, I’m just bullish.

I think we can very easily go lower and probably do see new lows sometime in the next few months, but I think we’re also pretty close to the bottom both price and time wise and this feels like an excellent time to be accumulating quality coins

If your time horizon is 1-3+ years, these dips are for buying

tweets.text:
If you’ve followed me over the years, you’ve seen me say this many times: you can often tell a lot about a person just by looking at them.

Not with certainty, but sometimes you see a CEO and think, “This person won’t be running the company in a few years,” or even, “This company won’t exist.”

Call it intuition, call it pattern recognition. Some people project competence, confidence, and leadership. Others don’t. And more often than not, those first impressions end up being closer to reality than people are willing to admit.

tweets.text:
Please, enough with asking people whose livelihood depends on Bitcoin going higher what they think Bitcoin will do next.

Their entire business, reputation, and wealth are tied to that outcome. What exactly do you expect to get from them? A balanced, unbiased opinion?

If you want objective analysis, start by looking beyond the people who are financially incentivized to be bullish.

tweets.text:
The greatest tragedy of Bitcoin is the opportunity cost.

A generation of pro-tech, pro-future investors spent the past year holding Bitcoin for dear life, following it blindly even as every thesis except scarcity collapsed before their eyes.

The irony is that these were exactly the people who should have made the most money from the AI boom. They understood technology. They understood exponential change. They were looking in the right direction, but at the wrong asset.

While they stayed glued to Bitcoin, companies like $MU, $SNDK, $NBIS, and other AI infrastructure winners they understood better than most delivered life-changing returns.

thread_tweets.text:
The fact that Bitcoin traded at $1,000 once doesn’t help today’s investor.

What matters is what it can do for you from here, not what it did for someone else in the past.

If a hedge fund manager compounded at 30% for a decade, then you invested and lost 40%, that track record isn’t doing much for you.
thread_tweets.full_text:

thread_tweets.text:
Typically? Summer typically starts in April.

You’re talking about a Bitcoin seasonality pattern that has occurred three times in its entire history as if it’s a guaranteed fact.

The intellectual rigor among Bitcoin holders seems to have disappeared. What was once a debate grounded in evidence now feels increasingly driven by belief, hope, and certainty.

Just because an asset has gone up for 17 years doesn’t mean it will continue to do so.
thread_tweets.full_text:

thread_tweets.text:
We shouldn’t be as foolish as the Bitcoin pundits who predict the future with such certainty.

Nobody knows what Bitcoin will do next. But what we can observe is that many of the fundamentals and narratives that originally drove Bitcoin’s appeal are no longer as strong as they once were.

tweets.text: Trainees must be open-minded; the process requires them to suspend their egos while they discover what they are doing well and what they are doing poorly and decide what to do about it. The trainer must be open-minded as well, and it's best if at least two believable trainers work with each trainee in order to triangulate their views about what the trainee is like. This training is an apprentice relationship; it occurs as the trainer and trainee share experiences, much like when a ski instructor skis alongside his student. The process promotes growth, development, and transparency around where people stand, why they stand where they stand, and what they can do about improving it. It hastens not just their own personal evolution but the evolution of the organization. #principleoftheday

tweets.text:
Peter Lynch's Magellan Fund returned 29.2% annualised (1977–1990). Yet the average investor in the fund made 7-8% CAGR!  

Why? They bought after strong rallies and sold after declines.  

A great strategy can be ruined by emotional decision-making. Process and discipline pay.

tweets.text:
One overlooked story at Build: Microsoft's small models are now dramatically cheaper and faster with the same quality as some leading models.

Same quality per token while having lower cost per token, means users get far more tokens for the same subscription! 

Unmetered intelligence is what happens when the cost per token at a given quality drops far enough that you stop counting.
tweets.full_text:
One overlooked story at Build: Microsoft's small models are now dramatically cheaper and faster with the same quality as some leading models.

Same quality per token while having lower cost per token, means users get far more tokens for the same subscription! 

Unmetered intelligence is what happens when the cost per token at a given quality drops far enough that you stop counting.

tweets.text:
When people say data centers use millions of gallons of water, they're describing an old technology. Before, water went into evaporative cooling towers. Warm water pulled heat off the AI chips, then evaporated into the air to shed it. It was effective, but it burned through fresh water continuously, which is where the headline numbers come from today around "data centers use a massive amount of water."

The data centers we revealed at Build today don't work that way. The cooling loop is closed. Water is added once during construction and recirculates indefinitely between the servers and the chillers. No evaporation, no fresh-water resupply!

Satya put the scale in plain terms: a full year of water use is roughly what a single restaurant uses.

Keep in mind that for us, every liter and every watt is an optimization target. The economics and the environment push in the same direction!
tweets.full_text:
When people say data centers use millions of gallons of water, they're describing an old technology. Before, water went into evaporative cooling towers. Warm water pulled heat off the AI chips, then evaporated into the air to shed it. It was effective, but it burned through fresh water continuously, which is where the headline numbers come from today around "data centers use a massive amount of water."

The data centers we revealed at Build today don't work that way. The cooling loop is closed. Water is added once during construction and recirculates indefinitely between the servers and the chillers. No evaporation, no fresh-water resupply!

Satya put the scale in plain terms: a full year of water use is roughly what a single restaurant uses.

Keep in mind that for us, every liter and every watt is an optimization target. The economics and the environment push in the same direction!
